title: QA: LSP server availability check uses 'which' subprocess (PATH hijack + cost)
description:
## Severity
Minor
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/tools/lsp.rs:101-110
## Violation
Checks whether the LSP binary is installed by spawning `which <binary>`. Same PATH-hijack class as kill.rs — `which` itself can be shadowed. Also: a subprocess fork+exec per LSP call just to probe a binary that will be exec'd a few lines later anyway is wasteful. The spawn call would have produced an equally useful error.

Quoted (lsp.rs:101-110):
```
    if Command::new("which")
        .arg(server_cmd)
        .output()
        .map_or(true, |o| !o.status.success())
    {
        return (
            format!("Language server '{server_cmd}' not found. Install it to use LSP features."),
            true,
        );
    }
```

`map_or(true, ...)` also fails OPEN on "which itself failed" — interpreted as "server not found" which is wrong but at least benign.
## Mandated refactor
1. Remove the `which` shell-out; let `Command::new(server_cmd).spawn()` produce the not-found error and map `io::ErrorKind::NotFound` into the user-facing message.
2. If a pre-check is genuinely needed, walk `PATH` ourselves (use the `which` crate which does this with no fork) and cache the result per binary.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
